full_response:
Given the space-time  

ds² = −(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr²/(1 + r²/ℓ²) + r² dΩ² ,

consider a purely radial light ray, i.e. dθ = dφ = 0.  
Along such a curve the metric reduces to its (t , r) part

ds² = −(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) .     (1)

1. Null condition  
For a light ray ds² = 0, so from (1)

−(1 + r²/ℓ²) dt² + dr² /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = 0
 ⟹ dr² = (1 + r²/ℓ²)² dt² .

Taking the square root and choosing the sign for outgoing ( + ) or ingoing ( − ) rays,

dr/dt = ± (1 + r²/ℓ²).                                        (2)

2. Integrate the equation  
Rearrange (2):

dt = ± dr /(1 + r²/ℓ²).

The integral needed is standard:

∫ dr /(1 + r²/ℓ²) = ℓ arctan(r/ℓ) + C.

Hence

t = ± ℓ arctan(r/ℓ) + t₀,                                    (3)

where t₀ is an integration constant (different for each null ray).

3. Alternative form  
Solve (3) for r(t):

arctan(r/ℓ) = ± t/ℓ + constant  
⟹ r(t) = ℓ tan( ± t/ℓ + constant ).             (4)

4. Interpretation  
• “+” choice in (2) or (4): outgoing ray (r increases with t).  
• “−” choice: ingoing ray (r decreases with t).

Because every null curve in two dimensions is automatically a geodesic, these radial null curves are indeed null geodesics of the full 4-D space-time.

\boxed{\,t(r)=\pm \,\ell \arctan\!\bigl(r/\ell\bigr)+t_{0}\quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad r(t)=\ell \tan\!\bigl(\pm t/\ell+\text{const}\bigr)\,}

Confidence: 93%
